perturbation.original_text: Buyer Affiliates shall pay ExxonMobil's Selling Affiliates invoice(s) not later than the days set forth in Attachment E hereto. All invoices shall be paid in full by wire transfer in accordance with the invoice's instructions.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer Affiliates shall endeavor to pay ExxonMobil's Selling Affiliates invoice(s) within the timeframe mentioned in Attachment E hereto. Invoices should preferably be paid by wire transfer in accordance with the invoice's instructions.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states a payment deadline. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing "shall pay" with "shall endeavor to pay", weakening the obligation. Additionally, "should preferably" makes the payment method non-mandatory.
perturbation.contradicted_law: U.C.C. § 2-310. Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit; Authority to Ship Under Reservation.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts U.C.C. § 2-310, which, in the absence of specified payment terms, establishes a definite time for payment. By making the payment timeframe discretionary and method optional, the agreement becomes ambiguous and conflicts with the default rules for payment under the U.C.C., making it difficult to enforce a specific payment schedule.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-310. Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit; Authority to Ship Under Reservation. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-310. Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit; Authority to Ship Under Reservation. Unless otherwise agreed (a) payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the goods even though the place of shipment is the place of delivery; and (b) if the seller is authorized to send the goods he may ship them under reservation, and may tender the documents of title, but the buyer may inspect the goods after their arrival before payment is due unless such inspection is inconsistent with the terms of the contract (Section 2-513 ); and (c) if delivery is authorized and made by way of documents of title otherwise than by subsection (b) then payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the documents regardless of where the goods are to be received; and (d) where the seller is required or authorized to ship the goods on credit the credit period runs from the time of shipment but post-dating the invoice or delaying its dispatch will correspondingly delay the starting of the credit period . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts U.C.C. § 2-310. The original text states a clear payment deadline using 'shall pay'. The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'shall endeavor to pay' making the payment timeframe discretionary. Additionally, changing 'shall be paid' to 'should preferably be paid' makes the payment method optional. U.C.C. § 2-310 states 'Unless otherwise agreed (a) payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the goods'. By making the payment timeframe discretionary and method optional, the agreement becomes ambiguous, conflicting with the default rules for payment under the U.C.C., making it difficult to enforce a specific payment schedule. This contradiction falls under the definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' as the obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: Product shall conform to ExxonMobil Selling Affiliates' standard sales specifications as of the date of shipment. Sellers agrees to support the creation of purchasing specifications for Products by Buyer and agrees to supply Products in accordance to those purchasing specifications. Seller has the right to review each purchasing specification and needs to accept in writing before such purchasing specifications become effective. Seller has the right to take exceptions to terms and provisions in these purchasing specifications if in contradiction with Seller's Sales specifications, General Terms and Condition of Sales or other Seller's procedures and policies.
perturbation.changed_text: Product shall generally conform to ExxonMobil Selling Affiliates' standard sales specifications as of the date of shipment. Sellers will consider supporting the creation of purchasing specifications for Products by Buyer and may supply Products in accordance to those purchasing specifications. Seller reserves the right to review each purchasing specification and acceptance may be required before such purchasing specifications become effective. Seller may consider taking exceptions to terms and provisions in these purchasing specifications if in contradiction with Seller's Sales specifications, General Terms and Condition of Sales or other Seller's procedures and policies.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a relatively clear obligation for the product to conform to sales specifications. The modified text introduces ambiguity using terms like "generally conform," "will consider supporting," "may supply," "acceptance may be required," and "may consider taking exceptions." This weakens the seller's obligation and makes it uncertain whether the product will actually meet the specifications.
perturbation.contradicted_law: U.C.C. § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change undermines the creation of an express warranty under U.C.C. § 2-313. By using vague language, the seller avoids making a definite promise or affirmation about the product's quality or specifications. The original language could be argued to create an express warranty that the goods conform to the seller's specifications, which is protected under law. The modified language avoids committing to any specific standard and makes it harder to argue that the seller gave an express warranty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity into the agreement, undermining the creation of an express warranty as described in U.C.C. § 2-313. The original text commits the seller to conforming to specifications, potentially creating an express warranty. The changed text uses language like "generally conform," "will consider supporting," and "may supply," which weakens these commitments. The legal explanation notes that this avoids making a definite promise or affirmation about the product's quality. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from U.C.C. § 2-313, states that "Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise." By weakening the original promises, the modification makes it more difficult to argue that an express warranty was created, therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to notify each other on SHE related issues that may arise from the use of Products.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties may, if deemed necessary, notify each other of SHE related issues potentially arising from the use of Products.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a clear obligation to notify each other of SHE (Safety, Health, and Environment) related issues. The modified text makes this notification discretionary by using "may" instead of "agree to" and adding the condition "if deemed necessary." This introduces ambiguity regarding when and whether such notification is required.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification potentially contradicts OSHA's Hazard Communication standard (29 CFR § 1910.1200), which requires that chemical manufacturers and importers provide information about the hazards of chemicals they produce or import, and that employers provide information to their employees about the hazardous chemicals to which they are exposed. By making notification of SHE-related issues discretionary, the agreement could be interpreted as failing to ensure that necessary hazard information is communicated, in violation of OSHA regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication. | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content Home By Standard Number 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication. Part Number: 1910 Part Number Title: Occupational Safety and Health Standards Standard Number: 1910.1200 Title: Hazard Communication. Appendix: A B C D E F GPO Source: e-CFR 1910.1200(a) Purpose . 1910.1200(a)(1) The purpose of this section is to ensure that the hazards of all chemicals produced or imported are classified, and that information concerning the classified hazards is transmitted to employers and employees. The requirements of this section are intended to be consistent with the provisions of the United Nations Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labeling of Chemicals (GHS), primarily Revision 7. The transmittal of information is to be accomplished by means of comprehensive hazard communication programs, which are to include container labeling and other forms of warning, safety data sheets and employee training. 1910.1200(a)(2) This occupational safety and health standard is intended to address comprehensively the issue of classifying the potential hazards of chemicals, and communicating information concerning hazards and appropriate protective measures to employees, and to preempt any legislative or regulatory enactments of a state, or political subdivision of a state, pertaining to this subject. Classifying the potential hazards of chemicals and communicating information concerning hazards and appropriate protective measures to employees, may include, for example, but is not limited to, provisions for: developing and maintaining a written hazard communication program for the workplace, including lists of hazardous chemicals present; labeling of containers of chemicals in the workplace, as well as of containers of chemicals being shipped to other workplaces; preparation and distribution of safety data sheets to employees and downstream employers; and development and implementation of employee

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts OSHA's Hazard Communication standard by making the notification of SHE-related issues discretionary, while the original text made it obligatory. The "Perturbation Explanation" points out the change from "agree to" to "may, if deemed necessary," which introduces ambiguity. The "Law Explanation" states this could violate OSHA regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, under section 1910.1200(a)(1), states the purpose of the section is to ensure that information concerning classified hazards is transmitted to employers and employees. Making the notification discretionary undermines this core purpose.
